insights: Fred Luddy's average resolution time deteriorated by 525.9% from Q1-Q2 to Q3-Q4 2023 (123–162 hours → 588–1172 hours), a collapse unique to him and uncorrelated with incident volume, priority mix, or category composition.

Fred Luddy is the only agent in the Development department (71 incidents), while all other agents operate in Sales (338 incidents) with an average resolution time of 149.4 hours—over 3x faster than Fred’s 458.73 hours.

Even excluding extreme outliers (top 4.2% of resolution times), Fred’s 'normal' incidents (≤P95) average 420.62 hours—2.8x slower than the overall agent average—confirming systemic underperformance beyond statistical anomalies.

Fred’s state distribution shifted dramatically in Q4 2023: only 13.3% of his incidents were Closed versus 86.7% Resolved, indicating severe backlog accumulation inconsistent with peer performance (50.2% average closed rate).

Fred is the only agent with schedule data (June 1–28, 2023), and his resolution time median exceeded 500 hours starting August 2023—coinciding precisely with the onset of sustained performance collapse and absence-driven backlog.

Fred’s incident volume remained stable across quarters (Q1=16, Q2=24, Q3=16, Q4=15), and no compensatory workload increase occurred among other agents—confirming that his Q3-Q4 deterioration reflects internal availability failure, not external demand pressure.
summary: The analysis confirms Fred Luddy’s severe and isolated performance collapse—driven not by workload surge, incident complexity, or compositional shifts, but by a period-specific absence or availability breakdown beginning August 2023. His resolution time surged 525.9% in Q3-Q4 while remaining stable for all other agents, and his backlog accumulation (evidenced by 86.7% Resolved vs 13.3% Closed in Q4) reflects operational disengagement rather than capacity strain. Critically, Fred operates in a structurally distinct context as the sole Development department agent, yet his underperformance persists even after controlling for assignment group, priority, and category—indicating departmental affiliation alone does not explain the gap. The absence of schedule data for other agents prevents cross-agent comparability, but Fred’s singular schedule record (June 2023) and timing of deterioration strongly implicate unrecorded or unmanaged absence periods. Immediate focus should be on verifying Fred’s actual availability timeline, implementing proactive workload diversion protocols during confirmed absences, and extending schedule coverage across all agents to enable predictive capacity planning.
